title: 14 · The Selection System
body:
ARMADA RECOVERY  ·  DETOX OF AKRON
THE SELECTION SYSTEM
How we choose the people who carry the standard
Select for who they already are. Teach the rest.
Version 1.0  ·  Built around the Hiring Scorecard

THE PHILOSOPHY
We select people. We don’t just hire them.
Horst’s hardest-won lesson: the qualities that make someone extraordinary with a frightened, sick, ashamed human being — warmth, patience, the refusal to judge — are largely formed long before they ever apply here, and they cannot be trained in afterward. So we don’t hire for a résumé and hope the character shows up. We select for the character that’s already there, and we teach the skills. Skills are cheap to add; character is impossible to install.
Why this is the most important system we have: every comfort, every Save, every send-off is delivered by a person we chose. We can write the finest standard in the field, but a patient only ever meets the people behind it. Get selection wrong and no document can save the experience.
And the cost of a wrong selection here is not just turnover — it’s a vulnerable patient cared for by the wrong hands. That is why we would rather hold a role open than fill it with someone who fails on character.
What we select for — the five core traits
These are the non-negotiable traits for every patient-facing role, support staff included. They’re scored on the Hiring Scorecard, and the rule is absolute: if a candidate falls below threshold on any one of them, it’s a no-hire — no matter how strong the résumé.
Trait
What it looks like
The red flag
Warmth
Genuine care for people; treats a stranger in crisis like a person who matters.
Cold, transactional, or only “professional” with no humanity.
Reliability
Shows up, follows through, can be counted on under pressure and at 3am.
A pattern of no-shows, excuses, or dropped commitments.
Non-judgment
Meets people with addiction without contempt or moralizing.
Stigma language; talks about “addicts” or “junkies” with disdain.
Purpose-fit
Genuinely wants to serve this population; the work means something to them.
Just needs a job; can’t say why this population, this work.
Coachability
Takes feedback, owns mistakes, wants to get better.
Defensive, knows-it-all, blames others when corrected.
Two more are threshold conditions assessed throughout, not scored on a curve: integrity (honesty, no shortcuts with vulnerable people) and emotional resilience (this work is heavy — relapse, AMAs, hard nights; we select people who can carry it without burning out or going cold).

The selection process — six steps, every time
The same consistent funnel for every candidate. Consistency is what makes it fair, legally sound, and predictive — not one manager’s gut on a busy day.
Step
What happens
Why
1. Screen
Recruiter/manager screens for knockouts: credentials, availability (incl. nights/weekends), and obvious fit.
Fast filter — don’t spend the team’s time on non-starters.
2. Behavioral interview
Structured, same-questions-for-all behavioral interview, scored on the Hiring Scorecard. Past behavior predicts future behavior.
The core of selection — reveals the five traits through real stories.
3. Team / peer interview
A second voice (a peer or the team they’d join) meets them. Never a solo decision.
Lateral fit, and a guard against one person’s blind spot or bias.
4. Realistic job preview
Show the hard truth — sick and sometimes angry patients, nights and weekends, people who leave and relapse — then ask if they still want it.
Lets the wrong person self-select out before day one, not at day sixty.
5. References & compliance
References, license/credential verification, healthcare background and exclusion screening, drug screen per policy.
Safety and credibility for a vulnerable population (run by HR/Compliance).
6. The decision
A short selection huddle aligns the interviewers against the scorecard. No-hire if any core trait is below threshold.
Deliberate, shared, standard-based — never a rushed solo call.
The interview is a significant emotional event
Horst insisted the interview itself signal that this is somewhere special. A candidate should leave feeling they encountered a real mission and a high bar — not a transaction. So in every interview we:
Lead with purpose. We open by telling them what we stand for and a real patient story — before we ask anything.
Make clear it’s selective. People value what’s hard to get into; we’re honest that we choose carefully because patients depend on it.
Treat the candidate the way we want them to treat patients — warmth, dignity, presence. The interview is the first lesson in the standard.

The behavioral question bank
Past behavior is the best predictor. Ask for real stories — “tell me about a time” — not hypotheticals, and listen for the trait, not the polish.
Trait
Ask them
Warmth
“Tell me about a time you cared for someone who was hard to care for.”  ·  “Describe going out of your way for someone who couldn’t repay you.”
Reliability
“Tell me about a time you showed up when it would have been easier not to.”  ·  “Describe a commitment you kept that cost you something.”
Non-judgment
“Tell me about working with someone whose choices you disagreed with — how did you treat them?”  ·  “What do you believe about people struggling with addiction?” (listen for stigma)
Purpose-fit
“Why do you want to work with people in recovery, specifically?”  ·  “Tell me about a time your work felt truly meaningful.”
Coachability
“Tell me about hard feedback you got — what did you do with it?”  ·  “Describe a time you were wrong and changed your mind.”
Resilience (scenario)
“A patient is yelling at you and says they’re leaving. Walk me through what you do.” (reveals warmth + composure + the Save instinct at once)
The role layer — on top of the five traits
Every role requires the five core traits first. Then each adds its own competency — but competency never substitutes for character.
Role family
On top of the five traits
BHT
The traits weigh heaviest of all — minimal credential, maximum character; they’re the 24/7 relational backbone.
Nurse / NP / Physician
Licensure and withdrawal-management competence — but they still must pass all five traits, no exceptions for clinical skill.
Case Management
Organization and communication with families, courts, and referral partners.
Peer Support
Lived experience plus stable recovery and strong boundaries (recovery-time guidance set with clinical/HR).
Housekeeping / Dietary
Fully patient-facing — the five traits apply completely; they shape the patient’s first impressions of care.
Leadership
Selected as leaders, not managers — see below.
Selecting leaders, not managers
Horst found only a handful of his people were true leaders. A manager administers; a leader creates vision, aligns and inspires people, develops them, and lives the standard under pressure. We select and promote leaders deliberately against that bar — a great BHT is not automatically a great leader, and we don’t promote on tenure alone.

Special considerations & compliance guardrails
Lived experience is an asset — with guardrails. Many of our best people are in recovery themselves. We value it, and we set fair, consistent expectations around recovery stability and boundaries with clinical and HR.
Fair-chance hiring, within safety limits. We don’t write people off for their past — we select for who they are now — within the legal and safety requirements for a vulnerable population. Decided case-by-case with Compliance.
Structured and consistent = fair and defensible. Same questions, scored the same way, multiple interviewers — this reduces bias and keeps us ADA/EEOC-compliant. Run the process with HR.
Credentialing, background, exclusion, and drug screening are mandatory for a healthcare setting — license verification, OIG/SAM exclusion checks, healthcare background checks, and drug screening per policy, handled with dignity.
Compensation must comply with EKRA. Separate from selection, but pay for clinical and business-development roles cannot be tied to patient volume or referrals — confirm any comp design with counsel.
None of the above is legal advice — the specifics are set with HR, Compliance, and counsel. The principle is simple: select for character, verify for safety, run it consistently.
How we know it’s working
Metric
Target
Quality of hire (90-day manager rating against the five traits)
≥ 90% meeting standard
90-day new-hire retention
Trend up
One-year retention
Trend up (turnover is the enemy of patient care)
Hiring-manager satisfaction with selected candidates
High
Roles held open rather than filled with a no-trait hire
A point of pride, not a failure
The discipline that protects everything: an empty chair is recoverable. A wrong hire in front of vulnerable patients is not. We select slowly and deliberately, because every person we choose becomes the standard a patient actually experiences.
The handoff — selection into orientation
Selection ends where the next significant emotional event begins. The moment we choose someone, they’re handed to an orientation built to make them feel they’ve joined something that matters — fully outfitted, welcomed by name, and launched into the Standard, not into paperwork. Selection and orientation are one continuous promise: we chose you carefully, and you belong here.
“The Selection System” · Armada Recovery · Detox of Akron · v1.0

title: The Table — Food & Snacks Standard
tags: akron standard, food, nourishment, the table
body:
ARMADA RECOVERY
THE TABLE
Our food, our snacks, and the promise no one here is ever hungry
Menu · seconds · service · access — across detox and residential
Version 1.0  ·  The operating companion to The Nourishment System

WHY FOOD MATTERS HERE
The most basic proof that someone cares.
Our patients arrive depleted — malnourished, dehydrated, running on nothing. For them, food isn’t a service line; it’s medicine and it’s comfort, and it’s often the first proof in a long time that somebody gives a damn whether they eat. “More food” is also one of the most common reasons a patient walks out — and the single most fixable one. So our stance is simple and total:
The promise: no one here is ever hungry — not at noon, and not at 2am. We serve abundance, not rations, within clinical and safety guardrails; we serve it with dignity; and hunger is never a reason anyone leaves.
And a hard-won principle: early recovery is not the time to be the food police. A body clawing its way back wants comfort and calories; we give nourishing options generously and we don’t shame anyone’s plate. Comfort first, nutrition alongside — never deprivation.
Meeting the body where it is
Food flexes with where the patient is in recovery. The medically complex cases — diabetic, severely malnourished or refeeding-risk, serious allergies — are flagged and managed with clinical and dietitian oversight (see guardrails). The general standard:
Stage
What the table looks like
Early detox
The body is sick — nausea, no appetite, GI distress. Gentle, bland, easy-to-tolerate options (broth, crackers, toast, bananas, oatmeal), small and frequent, heavy on hydration and electrolytes. Bring it to them when they can’t come to it.
Stabilizing
Appetite roars back — often intense, with strong sugar and carb cravings. This is real hunger from a healing body. Be generous. Protein, complex carbs, fruit, and yes, some comfort. This is where “more food” matters most.
Residential / settled
Patient is participating in the day. Hearty, balanced, communal meals on a steady rhythm — routine itself is stabilizing, and the table becomes a place of community, not just calories.

The rhythm of the day
Three meals, three snack windows, and an always-open station — a steady rhythm keeps blood sugar (and mood) stable, which prevents the crashes that drive people toward the door.
Time
Meal
Notes
7:00–8:30
Breakfast
Hot + cold options, eggs, oatmeal, fruit; coffee & tea
~10:00
Morning snack
Fruit, yogurt, grab-and-go
12:00–1:00
Lunch
Hot entrée + vegetarian option, sides, salad, fruit
~3:00
Afternoon snack
Protein + grab-and-go
5:00–6:00
Dinner
Hearty comfort entrée, sides, dessert
~8:00
Evening snack
Warm/comfort options
Overnight
Station open
Nobody is hungry at 2am — ever
All day
Beverages
Water, coffee/tea (decaf in evenings), juice, milk
The menu
A rotating 7–14 day cycle so it never gets stale — comfort food done well, balanced with fresh and lighter options, and always at least one familiar fallback (grilled cheese, PB&J, soup) for anyone whose stomach can’t face the entrée. A sample day:
Meal
What good looks like
Breakfast
Eggs (scrambled or to order), oatmeal, fresh fruit, yogurt, toast/bagels, cereal; coffee, juice, milk.
Lunch
A hot entrée (grilled chicken, pasta, soup + sandwich), a vegetarian option, a side, salad bar, fresh fruit.
Dinner
A hearty comfort entrée (baked ziti, roast chicken, taco night), a starch and a vegetable, a vegetarian option, and a dessert.
Always available
A familiar fallback (grilled cheese, PB&J, cereal, soup) for anyone who can’t stomach the meal.
Snacks — the station
A stocked, visible snack station is the backbone of “never hungry.” It carries real food, not just sugar — but it carries comfort too, because deprivation isn’t the goal.
Category
What’s stocked
Fresh
Whole and cut fruit, veggies and dip.
Protein
Yogurt, string cheese, cottage cheese, hard-boiled eggs, nuts, peanut butter, jerky, protein bars.
Grab-and-go
Granola bars, crackers, pretzels, cereal, oatmeal packets, trail mix.
Comfort
Cookies, pudding, popsicles/ice cream, chips — yes, some treats. Recovery isn’t the time for deprivation.
Hydration
Water, electrolyte drinks, juice, milk, coffee & tea (decaf available in the evening).

When can they get to it? — access
During the day: the station is open and accessible between meals — self-serve where it’s safe to be, or handed over warmly by staff. A patient should never have to “wait for the next meal” to eat.
Evenings and overnight: this is the danger zone — boredom, anxiety, and hunger are when people talk themselves toward the door. The station stays stocked and available all night; if it’s staff-accessed for safety, staff say yes immediately, no questions.
The rule: a hungry patient at any hour gets fed at that hour. “The kitchen’s closed” is not a sentence we say.
Can they have seconds? — yes.
Abundance is the default, not the exception.
Seconds are available at every meal, and portions are generous to begin with.
Staff are empowered to say yes to more food on the spot — no permission, no hoops. “Can I get more?” is answered with “Of course,” every time.
“More food” is never a reason a patient leaves. If hunger ever shows up in an AMA note, the system failed, not the patient.
The only “no” is a documented clinical restriction — a specific medical diet for a specific patient, set by clinical. That’s care, not rationing, and it’s explained with respect.
How it’s served
In a real dining space, plated and served with care — not a tray shoved through a slot. The way food is served is itself the message that the person matters.
Staff present and warm at mealtime. Meals are a community moment and a natural check-in on every patient.
In early detox, food comes to the patient where they are, gently, in small amounts they can manage.
The little things: remember a patient loves extra fruit and it’s already on the plate. That’s the difference between feeding someone and caring for them.
Special touches that say “you matter”
A cake for a sobriety milestone or a birthday — many of our patients haven’t been celebrated in years.
Real holiday meals; an occasional favorite request honored. Small, human, unforgettable.

Special diets & accommodations
Handled as standing accommodations through Dietary and the chart — never as an informal personal favor that disappears on someone’s day off.
Diet / need
How it’s handled
Gluten-free, vegetarian / vegan
Documented in the chart, flagged to Dietary, provided as a standing option every meal.
Religious (kosher / halal)
Accommodated and sourced as a standing requirement; treated with respect.
Food allergies
Documented, flagged, allergen-controlled in the kitchen — a safety issue, handled rigorously.
Diabetic / medically complex / refeeding risk
Managed with clinical and registered-dietitian oversight — the clinical team owns the medical specifics.
The guardrails
Clinical oversight for the medically complex (diabetic, refeeding risk, allergies) — owned by clinical and a registered dietitian, documented in the chart. Everyone else: abundance.
Safety & contraband: food service is never a vector for contraband; item and utensil handling follows the facility safety policy.
Sleep & comfort: decaf in the evenings; treats balanced so late-night sugar doesn’t wreck sleep — offered, not policed.
Food safety: standard temperatures, storage, and allergen control — always.
The one and only “no”: a specific, documented clinical restriction for a specific patient. Everything else defaults to yes.
How we know the table is working
Indicator
Target
“I never went hungry here” (patient survey)
100%
Food / dining survey top-box
High and rising
Food cited in AMA reasons
Zero
Special diets documented & accommodated
100%
Seconds available at every meal
Always
The table in one line: generous, comforting, dignified food a patient can get at any hour, served like they matter — because to a body and a soul that have been running on empty, a full plate is the first thing that says: you’re safe here, and you’re worth feeding well.
“The Table” · Armada Recovery · v1.0  ·  Set the medically-specific diets with clinical & a registered dietitian

title: The Documentation Standard
body:
ARMADA RECOVERY
THE DOCUMENTATION STANDARD
Why we document, the standard we hold, and how we help every clinician meet it
Documentation Excellence & Progressive Accountability Policy
DRAFT v0.9  ·  for Compliance, Clinical, HR & Legal review
Not yet in effect — timeframes and disciplinary mechanics to be confirmed before adoption

PART 1 · PURPOSE & PHILOSOPHY
Documentation is patient care — not paperwork.
A clinical note is not a bureaucratic chore. It is the next clinician’s eyes on the patient. It is the patient’s safety, their story, and the continuity of their care when the shift changes. It is the medical-necessity record that funds the treatment and keeps our doors open so we can keep helping people. And it is the protection — for the patient, the clinician, and the organization — that proves the care actually happened. A missing or late note is not a clerical slip; it is a defect in the clinical product, and our patients cannot afford defects.
How we hold this standard: cared for and pushed. We will set every clinician up to succeed — the right tools, the time, the training, and real support. We will recognize the people who do it well. When someone falls behind, we will first find and fix the real barrier. And we will hold a firm, fair, consistent line of accountability — because the standard is non-negotiable. Support and high standards are not opposites here; they are the same commitment.
Who this applies to
This policy applies to all clinical staff who create, contribute to, or are responsible for clinical documentation across all Armada Recovery sites and levels of care — detox, residential, and outpatient programming — including counselors, therapists, nurses, case managers, and medical providers, as scoped by their role.

PART 2 · THE STANDARD
What excellent documentation means here
Timely — completed within the required timeframe, while the service is fresh and the record is useful to the next person caring for the patient.
Complete — every required element present; nothing left for someone else to reconstruct.
Accurate & individualized — reflects the actual service and this specific patient, in person-first language, supporting medical necessity — never copied-forward boilerplate.
Compliant — meets Joint Commission, payer, state-licensing, and 42 CFR Part 2 / HIPAA requirements.
Required timeframes
Recommended starting framework. Clinical Directors and Compliance confirm the exact timeframes against payer, Joint Commission, and state requirements for each level of care before adoption.
Document
Recommended timeframe
Owner
Group / progress notes
By end of shift, within 24 hours of service
Counselor / BHT
Individual session notes
Within 24 hours of session
Therapist / Counselor
Admission / biopsychosocial assessment
Within [X] hours of admission
Clinician / Nursing
Treatment plan (initial)
Within [X] days of admission
Primary Clinician
Treatment plan reviews / updates
Per required schedule
Primary Clinician
Physician / medical documentation
Per medical staff rules & level of care
Provider
Discharge documentation
Within [X] days of discharge
Clinician / Case Mgmt

PART 3 · OUR SIDE OF THE DEAL
How we set you up to succeed
Before we hold anyone accountable, we owe them the conditions to meet the standard. These are the organization’s commitments — and a documentation problem is treated as a failure of these first.
Realistic timeframes built with clinicians, not imposed on them.
Time to document built into the schedule — charting time is work time, not something squeezed into unpaid margins.
Tools that work — a functioning EHR, good templates, and quick technical help when it breaks.
Onboarding that teaches the standard and the why, with competency confirmed before solo caseloads (see Training & Certification).
Supervisors whose first job is to remove barriers, not to build a file.
The system-first rule: if more than one clinician is falling behind in the same way, the problem is the system — the timeframe, the tool, the workload — not the people. We fix that before we escalate anyone.

PART 4 · PROGRESSIVE ACCOUNTABILITY
The defined steps
When a clinician falls behind, our first response is support and understanding the cause — not punishment. Accountability escalates only when the issue persists after real support. Every step is applied consistently to everyone, documented fairly, and conducted with respect.
Step 1 — Support & Root-Cause (first occurrence)
A prompt, private conversation between the clinician and supervisor — assuming good intent.
Purpose: understand why — workload, the tool, a training gap, an unrealistic timeframe, or a personal circumstance — and remove the barrier.
Review of expectations and the reason they matter; concrete support offered (training, protected charting time, EHR help).
Documented as a supportive coaching contact — this is help, not discipline. If it’s a system issue, it routes to a system fix.
Step 2 — Formal Corrective Action (repeated, after support)
Written corrective action documenting the pattern and the support already provided.
A Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) with specific, time-bound, achievable goals — and the support attached to each.
Increased documentation monitoring and scheduled check-ins; continued coaching, with HR involved.
Step 3 — Serious or Persistent Noncompliance
Suspension of scheduling privileges — a clinician who cannot keep a safe record should not carry a caseload until resolved.
Impact on performance evaluation.
Further disciplinary action up to and including termination, per organizational policy and in consultation with HR and counsel.
The exception to the ladder: serious single acts — falsifying a record, documentation that endangers patient safety, or fraud — are not “first-occurrence coaching.” They move directly to formal action under HR and legal guidance.

PART 5 · RECOGNITION
Catch people doing it right
A policy that only punishes failure moves the bottom; recognition moves the whole team. The thing that actually changes behavior is noticing and celebrating excellence.
We measure documentation timeliness and quality — and we recognize the clinicians who consistently nail it, in the daily lineup, in evaluations, and through the recognition system.
Documentation excellence is a factor in advancement and certification.
The goal is a culture where great documentation is normal and noticed — not one where bad documentation is merely caught.
Exceptions
The standard flexes, in good faith, for genuine circumstances:
Approved leave of absence.
Extended illness.
System outages or technical failures.
Other circumstances approved by management.
A two-way street: clinicians are responsible for notifying their supervisor promptly when something may prevent timely documentation — and the organization is responsible for flexing in good faith when they do. Communicating early is meeting the standard.

PART 6 · ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
Role
Responsibility
Clinician
Document to the standard; raise barriers early; communicate before a deadline is missed.
Supervisor / Clinical Director
Set the team up to succeed; run the root-cause conversation; remove barriers; apply the ladder consistently and with dignity; recognize excellence; document fairly.
Human Resources
Ensure consistent, lawful application; partner on corrective action, PIPs, and termination decisions.
Compliance
Monitor and audit documentation; align the standard with payer, Joint Commission, and state requirements; report patterns.
Leadership
Protect charting time and working tools; model the standard; treat repeated failures as a system signal first.
Definitions
Documentation: any clinical record a staff member is responsible for — notes, assessments, treatment plans, and related entries.
Timeframe: the maximum time allowed between a service and its completed record.
Corrective action / PIP: a formal, documented plan with specific goals, support, and a timeline.
Regulatory & policy references
To be confirmed with Compliance and counsel. Joint Commission documentation standards; payer and Medicaid medical-necessity and documentation requirements; applicable state licensing regulations; 42 CFR Part 2 and HIPAA for record handling. Related Armada policies: Leading the Team, The Safeguards, Training & Certification.

APPENDIX A · ACKNOWLEDGMENT
Clinician acknowledgment
I have received and reviewed the Armada Recovery Documentation Standard. I understand why documentation matters, what is expected of me, the support available to me, and how accountability is applied. I understand my responsibility to communicate promptly when a circumstance may prevent timely documentation.
Clinician name: ______________________________      Role: ____________________
Signature: ______________________________      Date: ________________
APPENDIX B · APPROVAL & VERSION CONTROL
Approver
Name / signature
Date
Clinical leadership

Human Resources

Compliance

Legal counsel

Version: DRAFT v0.9 · Effective date: ___________ · Review cadence: annually or upon regulatory change.

title: Running the Documentation Standard — Supervisor Playbook
body:
ARMADA RECOVERY
RUNNING THE DOCUMENTATION STANDARD
A playbook for Clinical Directors & supervisors
The policy is only as good as how consistently and humanely it’s applied. That’s you.
Companion to the Documentation Standard · DRAFT v0.9

THE MINDSET
You’re a leader, not an enforcer.
Your job is not to catch clinicians failing. It is to make it easy for them to succeed, and to hold a fair line when they don’t. Carry three things into every documentation conversation:
Assume good intent first. Almost no one wakes up planning to skip a note. Something is in the way — find it.
Root cause before consequence. If the system caused it, fixing the person won’t fix it.
Cared for and pushed. Warmth and a firm standard in the same conversation. That’s the whole job.
The Step 1 conversation — how to run it
This is the most important thing you do. Done well, most issues end here. A simple arc:
Open with care, not accusation. “I noticed your notes have been running behind this week — I’m not here to ding you, I want to understand what’s going on and help.”
Listen for the barrier. Let them talk. Run the checklist below in your head.
Name the why. Reconnect them to what the note is for — the next clinician, the patient’s safety, keeping the doors open.
Remove the barrier, out loud. Commit to a specific fix you own (protected time, EHR help, training) and a specific thing they own.
Document it as support. A brief, factual coaching note — what you discussed, what each of you committed to, by when. Not a disciplinary write-up.
The barrier checklist
If the cause is…
Then the fix is…
Workload / no time to chart
A scheduling fix — protected charting time. This is yours to solve, not theirs.
The EHR / a broken tool
Technical help, a better template, or escalate the tool problem.
A training or skill gap
Targeted retraining and a competency check — not discipline.
An unrealistic timeframe
Flag it up — the timeframe may need to change for everyone.
A personal circumstance
Compassion and the exceptions process — LOA, flex, early communication.
Genuine carelessness, after support
Now — and only now — the ladder moves to Step 2.
The pattern test: if several clinicians are behind in the same way, stop coaching individuals. You have a system problem. Escalate it — fixing the people will not fix the system.

APPLYING THE LADDER
Consistency is fairness
The fastest way to lose your team’s trust — and to create legal exposure — is to apply this unevenly. Protect the standard by protecting consistency:
Same behavior, same step, every person. No favorites, no exceptions for the high performer.
Document every step factually — dates, what happened, the support provided, what was agreed. Facts, not adjectives.
Loop in HR at Step 2, and before any suspension or termination. You don’t carry the disciplinary mechanics alone.
Never skip the support step (except for falsification, patient-safety endangerment, or fraud, which go straight to HR/legal).
Recognition is part of your job, not a nice-to-have
Name documentation excellence out loud in the daily lineup — specifically, by name, for the real behavior.
Weave it into evaluations and advancement — the people who carry the standard should rise.
Aim for a 5:1 feel — far more catching-it-right than catching-it-wrong. That ratio is what changes a culture.
Common scenarios
Situation
Your move
A strong clinician suddenly slips
Step 1, with extra curiosity — a sudden change usually signals a personal or workload event. Lead with concern.
“Everyone’s behind because the EHR is awful”
Believe them. This is a system fix and an escalation, not six coaching notes.
Repeat issue after real support
Step 2 — written corrective action + PIP with support attached. Bring in HR.
You suspect a note was falsified
Do not coach. Document the facts and go straight to HR and Compliance.
A clinician flags they’ll be late, in advance
That’s the standard working. Flex, support, no consequence.
Your north star in one line: make it easy to do right, notice it when they do, find the real reason when they don’t — and hold the line with respect when you have to.

title: The Dignity Kit — Policy & Checklist
tags: akron standard, dignity kit, discharge, send-off
body:
ARMADA RECOVERY · DETOX OF AKRON
THE DIGNITY KIT
Policy & checklist — what no one leaves without.
“No one leaves here uncared for — not even the ones who leave.”
Version 1.0 · part of The SendOff & Safe Departure standard · clinical items (naloxone) operate under medical protocols

1 · WHY
Why the Dignity Kit exists
How a person leaves is the last thing they remember about us — and the thing they tell others about. A patient who walks out with their belongings in a trash bag, no plan, and nothing in their hands has been told, wordlessly, that they did not matter. A patient who leaves with a packed kit, naloxone in their pocket, and “you are welcome back” in their ears has been told the opposite — even if they are leaving against advice.
The Dignity Kit exists so that the quality of a person’s departure never depends on which staff member is working, how the patient is leaving, or what hour it is. It is the same for a planned graduation and a 2 a.m. AMA. Especially the AMA.
The rule: no patient leaves this building — planned discharge or AMA, day or night — without a Dignity Kit in their hands and the departure checklist completed. No exceptions, including the patient who is angry, who won’t sign, or who is “just leaving.”
2 · WHAT’S IN IT
The Dignity Kit — contents checklist
Kits are pre-assembled and stocked at the front desk and the nursing station, so one is always within reach when a patient decides to go. Check each item as it is placed in the bag with the patient.
The bag itself
A real bag — a clean drawstring backpack or duffel with the Armada mark. Never a trash bag. The bag is the first message.
Safety — the items that can save a life
Naloxone (2 doses) + quick-start instructions — for every opioid-history patient, every departure, per medical protocol. The single most important item in the kit.
Overdose & withdrawal one-pager — plain-language warning signs, what to do, when to call 911, in large readable type.
Fentanyl test strips (where lawful and per protocol) with instructions.
Crisis & re-entry card — Armada’s 24/7 number (“come back any time”), 988, local crisis line, SAMHSA helpline.
Dignity — the items that say you matter
All personal belongings, returned in full — reconciled against the intake property log, including anything in the safe. Signed for.
Hygiene set — toothbrush, toothpaste, deodorant, soap, comb, feminine products as needed, travel-size and new.
Weather-appropriate basics — clean socks, a poncho or knit hat by season, and a clean t-shirt if theirs are soiled.
Food & water for the road — bottled water and shelf-stable snacks (protein bar, crackers). No one leaves hungry, either.
Phone charger / small power bank — a dead phone is a dangerous phone when someone is alone and struggling.
Continuity — the items that make a next step possible
Medication & prescriptions — any take-home meds, scripts, or MAT bridge per the provider; never withheld as leverage.
Discharge summary & medication list — a copy for the patient and, with consent, for their next provider.
Resource sheet — next appointment if scheduled, treatment/MAT referrals, housing and benefits contacts, AA/NA meeting list, transportation.
Recovery-capital items — their art or writing from the Restoration Rooms, a sobriety chip if earned, and a hand-written note from the team.
Consent / ROI offered — so we can make the follow-up call and coordinate care; offered, never required.

3 · HOW IT’S DONE
The departure ritual
A staff member sits with the patient and packs the kit with them — not handing them a pre-sealed bag, not having them wait at a desk. Packing it together is the goodbye.
Belongings are reconciled aloud against the property log so nothing is left behind — the reason a patient ever has to come back banging on a door is a kit done wrong.
Naloxone is placed in their hand with a 60-second teach-back — even in a doorway, even at an AMA, even if they’re in a hurry.
The last words are an invitation — “You are welcome back any time — tonight, tomorrow, whenever. This door stays open.”
The kit is logged and the departure charted the same shift — contents given, naloxone provided, classification correct (AMA vs. planned), follow-up call scheduled.
4 · OWNERSHIP & STOCK
Keeping kits ready
Par level: a set number of pre-assembled kits stocked at all times at the front desk and nursing station; checked and replenished every shift as part of the Handoff Standard.
Owner: the shift Crisis Owner confirms kits are stocked at lineup; nursing owns the naloxone and medication components under the Medical Director’s protocol.
Cost is not a reason to skip one. A complete kit costs a fraction of one readmission or one preventable death. If stock is out, assembling one now takes priority over the task in hand.
Measure: 100% of departures with a completed kit and documented naloxone, audited monthly on the Excellence Scorecard — the same number whether the patient graduated or walked.
The Dignity Kit · Armada Recovery · Version 1.0 · contents to be confirmed with Compliance, Nursing, and the Medical Director (naloxone, fentanyl test strips, and medications operate under medical and legal protocols).
